title: 28 — Respect for Human Life
html:
Big Question

Who is my neighbor? How far does my responsibility for the lives of others extend?

A man walks down from Jerusalem to Jericho. It is a dangerous road, winding through deep ravines. Thieves attack him, strip him, beat him half to death, and leave him. A priest passes by. He sees the body and crosses to the other side. A Levite, a Temple servant, a man devoted to God, passes. Same thing: he looks, quickens his pace, and moves on. Then a Samaritan comes along —a Samaritan, the enemy, the heretic whom no Jew would greet— and he stops. His heart is wrenched. He cleans the wounds with oil and wine, bandages them, lifts him onto his own animal, brings him to an inn, and pays for him. Before leaving, he tells the innkeeper: "Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I will repay you when I return."

Jesus tells this parable to a lawyer who had asked: "And who is my neighbor?" He ends with another question: "Which of these three, do you think, proved neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?" The lawyer replies: "The one who showed mercy on him." And Jesus says: "Go and do likewise" (Lk 10:36-37).

The fifth commandment, "You shall not kill," is not merely a prohibition. It is a positive command: respect life, defend it, care for it. Every human life, from conception to natural death, is sacred because God is its source and its end. The Good Samaritan does not ask whether the wounded man deserves help. He does not calculate whether stopping is convenient. He simply sees another human being who suffers, and acts. That is the Christian model: seeing Christ in the suffering, because "as you did it to one of the least of these my brethren, you did it to me" (Mt 25:40).

Activity

Close your eyes and imagine the Good Samaritan scene. The road, the sun, the dust. The wounded man. The priest passing by. The Levite also passing. And then the Samaritan who stops. Now ask yourself: who is the wounded person in my life today? It could be a classmate everyone ignores, an elderly family member who feels alone, a friend going through depression. Am I passing by, or am I stopping?

Draw or write: three concrete situations where you can be a "Good Samaritan" this week.

1. Only God is the Lord of Life

The fifth commandment is brief: "You shall not kill" (Ex 20:13). But its scope is immense. Behind these words lies the most fundamental truth: God is the sole owner of life. We did not give ourselves life; we received it. We did not choose to be born; we were called into existence by an act of the Creator's love. That is why no one has the right to arbitrarily dispose of human life —not one's own, nor anyone else's.

The Catechism states: "Human life is sacred because from its beginning it involves the creative action of God and it remains forever in a special relationship with the Creator, who is its sole end. God alone is the Lord of life, from its beginning until its end: no one can under any circumstance claim for himself the right directly to destroy an innocent human being" (CCC 2258). This truth does not depend on a person's health, productivity, age, or capacities. Life has value simply because it is human and because God loves it.

In Genesis we read that God demands an accounting for every human life: "For your lifeblood I will surely require a reckoning" (Gen 9:5). Every murder, every act of violence, every omission that leads to death is under God's gaze. The blood of Abel cries out from the earth. The blood of every innocent victim cries out as well.

Jesus Takes the Commandment to the Heart

In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus radically deepens the fifth commandment: "You have heard that it was said to the men of old, 'You shall not kill; and whoever kills shall be liable to judgment.' But I say to you that every one who is angry with his brother shall be liable to judgment" (Mt 5:21-22).

It is not enough to refrain from physical homicide. The root of murder lies in the heart: anger, hatred, contempt, envy, resentment. When we insult someone ("fool," "moron"), when we despise their dignity with our words, we are already killing in the heart. That is why the Lord adds: "If you are offering your gift at the altar, and there remember that your brother has something against you, leave your gift there before the altar and go; first be reconciled to your brother, and then come and offer your gift" (Mt 5:23-24). Reconciliation is a condition for worship.

Connection

Jesus does not simply prohibit killing; he commands love of enemies: "Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, so that you may be sons of your Father who is in heaven; for he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust" (Mt 5:44-45).

This is countercultural to the extreme. The world tells us: ignore those you do not like, hit back, do not forgive. Christ tells us: actively love the one who harmed you. Not because he deserves it, but because you are a child of a Father who loves without conditions. Who are you resentful toward today? Whom do you need to forgive —not with your feelings, but with your will— so you can approach the altar?

2. Legitimate Defense: Protecting Without Killing

The Church recognizes that it is licit to defend oneself and others against an unjust aggressor. "The legitimate defense of persons and societies is not an exception to the prohibition against the murder of the innocent... The act of defending oneself can have a twofold effect: the preservation of one's own life and the killing of the aggressor... The one is intended; the other is not" (CCC 2263).

This is crucial: the intention in self-defense is not to kill, but to protect life. If the aggressor's death results as an undesired effect (the principle of double effect), the action may be morally licit. But note: legitimate defense does not justify revenge or the disproportionate use of force. Only the force strictly necessary to repel the aggression may be used.

This applies also to law enforcement: police, military, security forces. They bear the grave responsibility of protecting innocent lives and may use proportionate force to do so. But they must never seek the aggressor's death as an end in itself.

The Death Penalty: Evolution of Doctrine

The Church's teaching on the death penalty has matured toward a position of abolition. In 2018, Pope Francis amended the Catechism to state: "The death penalty is inadmissible because it is an attack on the inviolability and dignity of the person" (CCC 2267, new text). The reason is clear: today means exist to defend society without executing the criminal. Life imprisonment protects the community while respecting the prisoner's life and leaving open the possibility of repentance.

Saint John Paul II had already moved in this direction in Evangelium Vitae: "Cases in which the execution of the offender is an absolute necessity are very rare, if not practically non-existent" (§56). Benedict XVI and Francis have consolidated this teaching.

Deep Dive

Saint Augustine and Saint Thomas Aquinas defended the liceity of the death penalty in certain circumstances. But they did so in contexts where secure prisons did not exist. The Church's doctrinal evolution shows organic development: the same truth (the inviolable dignity of the human person) is understood more deeply over time. This is how the living Tradition of the Church works.

It is not that the Church "changed its mind" arbitrarily. It is that, in the light of the Gospel of Life and new circumstances, she better understands what she has always known: that every human life is the image of God, even that of the worst criminal.

3. Abortion: A Grave Wound

The Church maintains, from the earliest centuries —the Didache (first century) already condemns it— that direct abortion, willed as an end or as a means, is gravely contrary to the moral law. "Human life must be respected and protected absolutely from the moment of conception. From the first moment of his existence, a human being must be recognized as having the rights of a person —among which is the inviolable right of every innocent being to life" (CCC 2270).

Modern science confirms what the Church has always taught: from fertilization, a unique human being exists, with its own complete genetic code, distinct from the mother and the father. It is not "part of the mother's body." It is not "an undifferentiated clump of cells." It is a human being in its first stage of development, deserving full protection. As Pope Francis writes: "It is not progressive to try to resolve problems by eliminating a human life" (Gaudete et Exsultate, 101).

Mercy for Mothers Who Have Had Abortions

It is crucial to understand the Church's stance: she condemns the act, not the person. Many women have abortions under extreme pressures —partner abandonment, economic precarity, family violence, fear— and afterward carry an immense burden of pain. The Church welcomes them with mercy. Pope Francis has insisted: "God's mercy reaches everyone." Priests may absolve the sin of abortion in the sacrament of Reconciliation, and ministries like Project Rachel exist to accompany healing from post-abortion wounds.

Saint John Paul II wrote to women who had had abortions: "Do not give in to discouragement and do not lose hope... The Father of mercies is ready to give you his forgiveness and his peace in the Sacrament of Reconciliation. You will come to understand that nothing is definitively lost and you will also be able to ask forgiveness from your child, who is now living in the Lord" (Evangelium Vitae, 99).

Witness: Saint Gianna Beretta Molla

Gianna Beretta Molla (1922-1962) was a pediatrician, wife, and mother in Italy. During her fourth pregnancy, she was diagnosed with a uterine fibroma. The standard medical treatment would have been a therapeutic abortion (removing the uterus with the child inside). Gianna refused: "If you must decide between me and the child, do not hesitate: choose the child. I demand it: save him." She underwent surgery that attempted to save both, but which carried grave risk for her. She gave birth to a healthy daughter, Gianna Emanuela, and died a week later from peritonitis. She was 39.

Saint John Paul II canonized her in 2004. Her husband and children were present at the ceremony —the first time a husband attended his wife's canonization. Gianna did not seek death; she chose her daughter's life because she knew that human life is sacred from the womb.

Today her daughter Gianna Emanuela is a geriatric physician who dedicates her life to caring for the elderly. From that act of love emerged a vocation.

4. Euthanasia and Care for the Sick

Euthanasia, defined as "an action or an omission which of itself or by intention causes death, in order that all suffering may in this way be eliminated" (CCC 2277), constitutes murder gravely contrary to the dignity of the human person. It is not an act of compassion; it is an act that eliminates the suffering person, mistakenly believing that this is the solution to suffering.

True compassion does not eliminate the one who suffers; it eliminates the suffering of the one who suffers, or accompanies them through it. Palliative care —pain control, psychological and spiritual accompaniment, loving presence beside the patient— is the genuinely human and Christian response. As Mother Teresa of Calcutta said: "The one who is alone, the one who is unwanted, the one who is unloved, is the poorest of the poor."

Ordinary and Extraordinary Means

The Church does not demand that life be prolonged at all costs. She distinguishes between ordinary means —nutrition, hydration, basic care, proportionate treatments offering reasonable hope— which must always be provided, and extraordinary or disproportionate means —treatments that are very costly, painful, or have little chance of success— which may be lawfully discontinued (CCC 2278-2279). Renouncing disproportionate treatment is not euthanasia; it is accepting the mortal condition of the human being, allowing death to follow its natural course.

Pope Pius XII declared in 1957 that there is no moral obligation to use extraordinary means. What is never licit is directly causing the patient's death, even at their request.

Suicide

Suicide is objectively gravely contrary to justice, hope, and charity. Each person is responsible for his life before God, who has given it (CCC 2281). Nevertheless, the Church recognizes that moral responsibility may be diminished or even nullified by serious psychological disturbances, anguish, fear, or extreme pressures. That is why the Catechism adds: "We should not despair of the eternal salvation of persons who have taken their own lives. By ways known to him alone, God can provide the opportunity for salutary repentance. The Church prays for persons who have taken their own lives" (CCC 2283).

This is of enormous pastoral importance. When a young person dies by suicide, or a family member, we Catholics must not condemn them, but entrust them to God's infinite mercy and pray for them. The Church no longer denies Christian funerals to those who have taken their own lives.

5. Respect for Souls: Scandal

An aspect of the fifth commandment often forgotten: one can kill not only the body but also the soul. Jesus used harsh words for scandal: "Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened round his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea" (Mt 18:6). Scandal is the action or omission that leads another to sin. It is a form of spiritual homicide.

The Catechism speaks clearly about the respect due to souls: "Scandal is especially grave when it comes from those who, by their nature or function, are charged with teaching and educating others" (CCC 2285). Parents who give bad example, priests who do not live their vocation, teachers who corrupt, influencers who promote destructive lifestyles —all bear a special responsibility before God.

Respect for the Dead

The Church surrounds death with dignity. Christian burial —giving Christian burial to the deceased— is a corporal work of mercy. The bodies of the dead must be treated with respect, for they were temples of the Holy Spirit. Cremation is not prohibited (so long as it is not chosen for reasons contrary to the faith, such as denying the resurrection), but the Church prefers burial, which better imitates Christ's burial and expresses hope in the resurrection of the body (CCC 2300-2301).

Biblical Connection: The Good Samaritan Today

"A Samaritan, as he journeyed, came to where he was; and when he saw him, he had compassion, and went to him and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine; then he set him on his own beast and brought him to an inn, and took care of him. And the next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper, saying, 'Take care of him; and whatever more you spend, I will repay you when I come back.' Which of these three, do you think, proved neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?"

Luke 10:33-36

The Good Samaritan parable is Jesus' answer to the most practical question of Christian morality: "Who is my neighbor?" And the answer is: the one who needs you. The Samaritan was not a canonized saint; he was a heretic in the eyes of Jews. But when he saw suffering, he did not ask whether the man was one of his own. He simply loved.

What does this look like today? The neighbor is the unborn life in danger. The neighbor is the elderly person in a nursing home whom no one visits. The neighbor is the terminally ill patient offered euthanasia as a "solution." The neighbor is the migrant crossing the border. The neighbor is the child with Down syndrome the world calls "a mistake." Each of them is Christ asking for help.

For Reflection

  1. Who is the "wounded person on the road" in your life today? Are you passing by or stopping?
  2. Jesus says that anger and insult are already a form of killing in the heart (Mt 5:21-22). Is there someone you need to truly forgive —not in lip service, but in the depths of your heart?
  3. The Church defends life from conception to natural death. Have you ever paused to think about why this is so radical in today's world? What challenges do you see to living this teaching in your environment?
  4. If a friend told you that euthanasia is "compassionate" because it eliminates suffering, how would you explain the difference between killing the sufferer and accompanying the sufferer?
  5. The Catechism tells us not to despair of the salvation of persons who have taken their own lives (CCC 2283). Do you know someone who has lost a loved one in this way? How might you be an instrument of God's mercy for that family?

Key Idea

Key Idea: Every human life is sacred from conception to natural death, because every person is made in the image of God. The fifth commandment is not merely "you shall not kill": it is a call to be the Good Samaritan, to see Christ in every suffering person, and to actively defend life in all its stages. God alone is the Lord of life. We are its stewards.

Cultural Riches

"The Good Samaritan" by Vincent van Gogh (1890) — Painted in the Saint-Rémy asylum where Van Gogh was recovering from mental crises, this work shows the Samaritan lifting the wounded man onto his horse. The vibrant colors and nervous brushstrokes convey the urgency of mercy. Van Gogh, who knew suffering firsthand, captured the physical, effortful compassion —the Samaritan does not have a serene face, but the tense face of someone exerting himself to help. Mercy costs.

Evangelium Vitae by Saint John Paul II (1995) — This encyclical is the Magna Carta of the defense of life. Its title says everything: "The Gospel of Life." John Paul II traces the biblical teaching on life, analyzes contemporary threats (abortion, euthanasia, death penalty), and proposes a culture of life over a culture of death. The introduction (nn. 1-6) and the letter to women who have had abortions (n. 99) are essential reading.

Man's Search for Meaning by Viktor Frankl (1946) — This book, written by a Jewish psychiatrist who survived Auschwitz, is not a Catholic text but resonates powerfully with the Church's teaching. Frankl discovered that human life has meaning even in the worst circumstances —that dignity does not depend on health, external freedom, or utility. His "logotherapy" is an existential argument against every form of contempt for "non-productive" life.

"The Scream" by Edvard Munch (1893) — This expressionist work shows a figure consumed by anguish beneath a blood-red sky. It is the image of human suffering that cries out to heaven. The Church does not ignore that cry: she hears it and accompanies it. But she points out that eliminating the one who cries out is not the solution; the solution is to embrace them and give them reasons to go on living.
